user_prompt: I want to develop a microphone gain manager, using `lib\AudioManager\MicGainManager.cpp` and `lib\AudioManager\MicGainManager.h`. There only needs to be one instance. The microhpone stream is assumed to be mono. The bit depth is always 16 bit PCM just like the rest of the project. The sample rate does not matter.

There should be a public facing initialization function and a "process" function that acts in-place on a buffer. There is a privately kept set of state variables. Also publically there are some getters and setters as needed.
All functionality related to tracking mic peak will now be moved to `MicGainManager`
The initialization will set an initial mic gain of 1.5x, note that a test showed that actually screaming into the mic results in about 75% full scale peak.

There is a target-gain, a current-gain, and a previous-gain. The current-gain can go up or go down once per call of process. The rate it goes up is slower than the rate it can go down (it's more important to respond to clipping).

The difference between current-gain and previous-gain is that, at the begining of "process", it uses previous-gain until a zero-cross event, then it uses current-gain and updates previous-gain. And then calculate the current-gain according to target-gain.
The mic peak is tracked using the raw unmodified values. The peak being tracked decays using the same method we use now, but called from within "process".
The target-gain is continously set to keep the mic peak at about 75% of full scale. It is clamped between 1x and 3x.
The mic peak is publicly readable so the GUI can display the peak, the mic peak after scaling with the current setting is also publicly available.
There is also to be a fixed gain mode, for when users manually control the gain. And there is to be a bypass mode.

The way the sample manipulation should work is: cast the sample to 32 bit, the 32 bit sample is multiplied by a gain, and then divided by a divisor, then written back into the original buffer as 16 bit. The gain scale is thus controlled by the divisor. We can start with a divisor of 128, and so the gain step of 1 is achievable and if my calculations are correct:

Sample rate is 16000 Hz, the I2S read buffer is set to 240 samples, so there are 66.67 calls of "process" per second. If we use 128 as the divisor, and want to double the gain automatically as slow as possible, we can go from 128 to 256 in about 2 seconds. And using 128, a power of 2, gives the compiler a chance to use bit shifting for the division. And 128 is decent resolution such that humans cannot detect any jarring transitions.
Warn me if my logic is flawed.
agent_reply: - Implemented in-place 16-bit mono PCM processing in [MicGainManager.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/MicGainManager.cpp:184).
- Moved mic peak tracking out of `AudioManager`.

- Wired `AudioManager::pump_mic2bt()` to process mic samples before queueing them in [AudioManager.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:643).
Your divisor math is good: `240` samples at `16000 Hz` is `66.67` process calls/sec, so `128` one-unit gain steps takes about `1.92s`.
- Initial `1.5x` gain means a raw `75%` full-scale scream can clip at the output before AGC pulls down.
- During real active silence, auto gain will drift toward `3x`; that can make the first loud transient hot. I prevented no-sample/idling calls from ramping gain.
- Zero-cross-only gain switching can delay gain changes if the mic signal has DC offset or very few crossings. A timeout fallback may be worth adding later.

agent_reply: Yep, that was my miss. `esp_task_wdt_reset()` only works for tasks registered with the watchdog, and `loopTask_core0` wasn’t. Worse, the actual watchdog failure was `IDLE0`, meaning the core-0 loop was yielding but never blocking long enough for the idle task to run.
That gives `IDLE0` time to run and removes the noisy “task not found” reset calls.

The crash was happening before app startup, inside ESP-IDF flash initialization. The decoded trace pointed at `esp_flash_read_chip_id()` calling `memcpy` while flash cache was disabled. The problem was that `memcpy` had been linked into flash at `0x401f...`, so the CPU faulted with:
`Cache disabled but cached memory region accessed`
I added a small linker override component:
That keeps only the needed libc memory/string routines in IRAM without turning the full PSRAM workaround back on. After a clean rebuild, `memcpy` moved to IRAM:

I also set `CONFIG_SPIRAM_CACHE_LIBMEM_IN_IRAM=y` in `platformio.ini` and `sdkconfig.defaults`, but the linker fragment is the real fix here because that sdkconfig option is gated by the older PSRAM workaround path.

user_prompt: Implement `lib\AudioFileRecorder\AudioFileDecoder.cpp` and its associated header file. It should be a class object
The constructor dictates what the input file name is, and registers callbacks
The class itself has public functions for
- the destructor must clean up correctly, there will be FIFOs involved, larger than what the stack allows
The start function will spin up a FreeRTOS thread, the thread is private, give it a stack size of 2048
the on-complete callback must be fired from poll, not the new thread
all actual file and file system operations must be contained within the new thread
the decoding strategy is a bit simpler than the python script

instantiate two AudioFIFO instances, each with 2048 capacity. One for left channel and one for right channel. The rule is: `file_packet_t` has a field `src`. If `src` is an odd number, the data goes into the left channel (this should be mic), if `src` is an even number, the data goes into the right channel (this should be BT).
The FIFO’s watermark feature should be used, both set to 512 samples (1024 bytes).

open the output file as “temp.wav”, write a fake WAV file RIFF header inside, once we are finished, this will be overwritten. make sure the file write seek pointer is now at the start of stereo 16 bit PCM data.
If both FIFOs can accept at least 512 more samples, then decode a packet, and enqueue it into the correct FIFO. (reminder that the maximum samples per packet is 256, although a size of 240 is likely)

Then, remembering that the watermark feature is used, we have three possible conditions: if only the left FIFO allows for dequeuing, if only the right FIFO allows for dequeuing, or if both FIFOs allow for dequeuing.
If both can be dequeued, then do the dequeue 128 samples from each at a time, interleaved into stereo PCM data and written to the output file.
If only one FIFO can be dequeued, then dequeue 128 samples and create stereo PCM data with the other channel silent, write it to the output file
If the file has been completely read to end, then set the watermark to 0 and continue dequeuing until both FIFOs are empty.
Then the loop is finished. Write the RIFF header correctly. Close the file “temp.wav” and then rename it to the proper name.
Set a flag saying it is complete. Break out of the loop. Delete the thread.

Progress report call back is called every 500ms, but also, only check if that time has elapsed every 8 packet being decoded. Progress is reported by the input file seek pointer vs the input file size.
A cancel flag means the temporary file is deleted and then the loop exits, the thread then ends correctly

user_prompt: we are making a big change
for unencrypted audio recordings, instead of recording to a custom format file with a `*.rec` extension, instead, we will record to a plain `*.wav` file extension.
we will do some things to make it easy to assemble a stereo 16 bit PCM file in real time even though the mic is optionally on or off and the bluetooth data might be unreliable as well.
first, I've already asked you to add a `queueSilence` to the `AudioFifo`. This is useful for the next change
when writing to the bt2file FIFO, if we know that the mic is off, then we insert silence into mic2file automatically.
when writing to the mic2file FIFO, and we see the bt2file FIFO is significantly behind, then we insert some silence to let it catch up.

when writing to the file, we only write if both FIFO has data so that the data can be mixed into stereo. Since the enqueuing mechanism now attempts to keep both FIFOs at the same level, this should work.
When opening the file, since it is a `*.wav` file in this case, we put a placeholder for the RIFF header. When the file is about to be closed, we put in the correct data into the RIFF header

user_prompt: you said `I also made playback mix stereo to mono before feeding the speaker FIFO, so overlapping mic/BT channels won’t silently drop the right channel.`
can you track which channel has more recent consecutive 0s, and if both are active, do a traditional mix, and if only one side appears active, take that side?
agent_reply: Done. I changed the WAV fold-down logic in [WavPlayback.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WavPlayback/WavPlayback.cpp:124).
Now it tracks consecutive exact-zero samples per channel:
- If both channels look active, it does the normal `(left + right) / 2` mix.
- If only left looks active, it takes left.
- If only right looks active, it takes right.
- If both look inactive, it outputs silence.
- Activity resets on seek/scrub so it re-detects cleanly after jumping.
I used an `8` sample zero-run threshold, so ordinary waveform zero crossings should not make a channel look inactive, but inserted silence should be detected quickly.
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec0`, and it passed.
